text: We are pleased to present you this video vignette of injection of mesenchymal stem cells, DARVAT, throstle, into perineal fistula of Crohn's disease performed by our team in Saint-Joseph Hospital in Paris. We present you the case of a twenty-year-old woman with Crohn's disease complicated by a high trans-sphincteric anal fistula since two thousand and fourteen. She had previously been treated with biologics and already had two sphincter sparing techniques. However, her anoperianal fistula was still evolutive. Anoperineal MRI confirmed the absence of residual suppuration and colonoscopy confirmed the absence of active luminal disease. She also underwent an exploratory procedure under anesthesia that confirmed good drainage and allowed performing cortage. Hence, we decided to treat this fistula with an injection of mesenchymal stem cells.

The MRI images show a high posterior trans-sphincteric anal fistula, indicated by a white arrow, filled with granulation tissue and lacking a liquid component. A yellow arrow highlights pre-coccygeal focal inflammation without purulent collection. The patient is prepared for the procedure in the lithotomy position.

The surgeon disinfects and cleans the perianal area using soap and water, revealing the fistula with a posterior internal opening and high trans-sphincteric tract. Curettage is performed using endoscopic brushes to remove granulomas and clean the fistula tract, while the seton remains in place to maintain visibility of the internal opening. The internal opening is closed using a circle needle with absorbable synthetic monofilament sutures, creating a hermetic seal with three interrupted sutures before removing the seton.

The surgeon prepares mesenchymal stem cells for injection by gently resuspending the cells in four vials, each containing thirty million cells. The vials are inverted, and the product is carefully extracted using an eighteen gauge needle to prevent cell lysis. The cells are then divided into four screw-type syringes of ten milliliters each, ensuring gentle handling to maintain cell integrity.

The surgeon begins the slow injection of mesenchymal stem cells into the internal opening using a long, 21-gauge green needle. Several injection points are used to prevent loss of the product due to increased pressure. Half of the dose is injected into the internal opening and the other half into the fistula tract, with administration from the external opening directed towards the internal opening. The injection is made approximately 2 to 3 millimeters deep into the walls of the fistula. The injected area is softly massaged, and the external opening is covered with a small adhesive bandage.
